translation.content: Even if you've never met Adam Longman Parker, AKA Afriqua, you can get to know him pretty well through his music. His lyrics ("Would you house me in a house, would you house right in my mouth?" from Dr. House), track titles ("Wagwan Bhagwan?") and cover art exude a charmingly cheeky demeanour that makes his music so personable. It helps that his latest records are absolute smashers–funk bombs with jacked beats and bouncy grooves. Once ensconced in the minimal world, the Virginia-born artist gravitated towards Miami bass, Midwest house and zesty techno before the pandemic. Thankfully so. "MAXI SINGLE", "Take It 2 Da House" and most of his recent discography combine Moodymann's slinky swagger with The Neptunes' killer sense of rhythm. Just like those legendary acts, Parker carries weight in both the underground and mainstream—he's released with Tomorrowland label CORE Records and DJ'd at Ibiza superclubs, all the while still appealing to more headsy crowds. His career expansion hasn't cost him his principles, either. A champion of Black history and culture (he wrote a five-part essay called "Principles of Black Music"), the classically trained pianist infuses overlooked Black musical traditions like psyfunk into his work. Colored, his debut full-length, was a tribute to what he called the "Black musical tree." "Genre is just temporary housing," the now Berlin-based producer noted in a recent Instagram post. That mentality informs his RA Podcast. From '90s-inspired house and luxurious harp melodies to some of his originals and even a "Satisfaction" remix, it's a celebration of unpretentious, feel-good music. To put it simply, it slaps—hard. – Nyshka Chandran What have you been up to recently? I just got back from Los Angeles, after a run of shows in the States. My girl and I were there scoping it out for a potential move and doing some sessions but also ended up experiencing the worst wildfires in their history. It's heartbreaking to see so many lives upended and so much musical history destroyed, from Schönberg to Madlib. It was a surreal experience, but also inspiring to see how the community came together to support one another. Thankful to our friends who took care of us and checked in while we were out there. Undeterred from becoming Angelenos ASAP. Big ups the Los Angeles Fire Department. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? At my homie Big Farma's 50th birthday last October on two CDJs, one Vestax PDX-2000 and some rotary whose name I forgot. Chopped out the best sections of the original seven-hour recording, and spliced them together. Polished it a bit in Pro Tools at the crib for home consumption. Farma has been with me for every step of my journey, so the idea was really just celebrating the big homie, and translating the vibe of that special night. Acid saké hit different. What are you looking forward to in the near future? Dropping collaborations with new dance artists who are fucking the game up right now—and who I'm learning a lot from in the process. I produced an album for Alina Pash last year and am excited about her upcoming stuff. Got some crazy Afriqua records in the pipeline too, of course. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? It's too easy to get lost in the sauce trying to solve problems beyond your sphere of influence to the neglect of yourself and your loved ones. Pay less attention to causes and more to positive daily action. Do great work, buy your shorty flowers and be nice to your parents. I already see this shift happening in our scene, and it's encouraging after the years of pettiness that backdropped the first part of my career. On that note, it would be dope if y'all at RA would finally make up with major contributors to culture over some bullshit. Let's show the younguns that millennials can be chill, make amends and not erase the legacies of musicians. Jackmaster should be a reminder that we need to take better care of our people. Music is redemptive if you let it be. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? Seven Deadly Cities in Virginia in 2001. It was the first B-boy battle I ever attended with my brother, where I also met Pharrell. Discovering DJing and production in the same night is hard to beat. I peaked at nine.
translation.blurb: Funked-up stompers from a crossover favourite.
